Conditions A to Z
Bowel polyps 
Bowel polyps are small growths on the lining of the large intestine (colon) or rectum. They're very common and not usually serious, but sometimes they can lead to bowel cancer. 
Bowel polyps are also called colonic or colorectal polyps.
Symptoms of bowel polyps 
Bowel polyps do not usually cause any symptoms, so most people with polyps will not know they have them.
They are often found during a colonoscopy , for example if you're being checked for another bowel condition, like bowel inflammation or cancer.
Rarely, larger polyps can cause symptoms such as:
a small amount of slime (mucus) or blood in your poo bleeding from your bottom a change in your normal bowel habits, such as diarrhoea or constipation losing weight without trying 
Non-urgent advice: See a GP if:
you have slime (mucus) or blood in your poo for 3 weeks or more your bowel habit is different to normal you are constipated and it's not improving with treatment, or it happens regularly and lasts a long time you've lost a noticeable amount of weight without trying Try not to be embarrassed. The doctor or nurse will be used to talking about these symptoms.
